demographics: I am 21 years old. I am female. I am White. I have never been married. I had 2 years of college education. My family income is $90,000 to $109,999. I am a not very strong Republican. I am moderate (middle of the road). My religion is Protestant. I attend religious services 2-3 times a month.

events.event_text: Last year, when I was 20, my church went through a scandal when it was revealed that our youth pastor had been embezzling money. It was devastating to see our community shaken like that.
[Step 2 skipped: constraint could not be satisfied — Biological incoherence: child_or_close_family_member_comes_out_as_lgbtq requires age ≥ 30, got 17]

I was 17 when my family moved from our small, predominantly white town to a more diverse city for my dad's job. I joined a church there that was much more inclusive and progressive than what I was used to. It really opened my eyes to different perspectives and helped shape my moderate views.

At 18, I decided to start volunteering at a local community center, a decision encouraged by my new, progressive church. This led me to interact with people from diverse backgrounds, providing me with a new perspective on life and pushing me to engage more with my community.
At 18, I was robbed at gunpoint while working the late shift at the local convenience store. The shocking experience made me reevaluate my feelings about gun control.

At 19, I decided to join a local protest against police brutality, sparked by a string of incidents in our city. It was my first time engaging in activism, and I found it both scary and empowering to stand up for what I believe in.

At 19, I welcomed my first roommate from a different racial background, a friend from college. It's been an eye-opening experience, learning about their culture and discussing our perspectives on life and society.

At 16, I faced discrimination while applying for a part-time job. I was the only applicant who wasn't hired, and the manager implied it was because of my religion. It was a harsh reality check that made me more aware of religious discrimination.

compressed_summaries.summary: This person, at 20, witnessed their church's community shaken by a youth pastor's embezzlement scandal. At 17, they moved to a more diverse city, joining a progressive church that broadened their perspectives. At 18, they started volunteering at a community center, fostering interactions with diverse individuals and encouraging community engagement.

This person, at 18, was robbed at gunpoint, sparking a reevaluation of their stance on gun control. At 19, they joined a protest against police brutality, finding it both intimidating and empowering to engage in activism for the first time. Around the same age, they welcomed a roommate from a different racial background, fostering cultural learning and perspective exchange.

persona_card: This 21-year-old White Protestant woman is moderately religious, attending services a few times a month, yet holds progressive views on moral issues, with a low score on moral traditionalism. She supports economic redistribution and believes in an active government role, while also being class-conscious, having experienced economic privilege. Her racial attitudes are strongly structural, recognizing systemic inequalities, yet she maintains a degree of openness towards outgroups, scoring moderately high on outgroup affect. Despite her progressive leanings, she has low trust in institutions, potentially influenced by her experiences with corruption and activism.
